findings.description: Session tokens are generated with the non-cryptographic `random` module (Mersenne Twister), whose internal state is recoverable from observed outputs, enabling token prediction and session hijacking / auth bypass (CWE-330/CWE-338, OWASP A07); use `secrets`/`os.urandom`.

`touch()` slides an expired-but-not-yet-purged session's expiry forward without checking `expires_at`, resurrecting it and defeating the TTL/idle-timeout control (CWE-613 Insufficient Session Expiration).

`user_sessions()` reads `self._sessions` without purging, so it can return expired sessions that callers may treat as valid (inconsistent with `get()`, which purges first) — expired-credential exposure (CWE-613).

Expiry purge runs only lazily on `get()`/`active_count()` and there is no per-user session cap, so create-heavy or read-free usage allows unbounded session/memory growth (CWE-770 resource exhaustion / DoS).

The `_sessions` dict is mutated without synchronization; concurrent create/destroy/purge can race (e.g., `del self._sessions[t]` after a concurrent pop raises KeyError) — robustness/availability concern, defer to logic critic for detail.
